past_medical_history: Hyperlipidemia, on statin therapy for 12 years
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 10 years ago, on oral agents
Coronary artery disease with two-vessel CABG 6 years ago
Mild aortic stenosis identified on echocardiogram 2 years ago
Chronic kidney disease stage 3a (baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL)
Remote history of minor head trauma from a fall 4 months ago without loss of consciousness
family_history: Father died of sudden cardiac death at age 65
Mother had hypertension and stroke, died at age 80
Brother diagnosed with complete heart block requiring pacemaker at age 70
No family history of seizure disorders

social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, 25-pack-year history, quit 15 years ago
social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinker, approximately once monthly
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a ground-floor apartment
social_history.exercise: Previously walked 30 minutes daily, has reduced activity over past 6 weeks due to increasing fatigue and lightheadedness

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech is fluent with normal rate, rhythm, and prosody. No dysarthria or aphasia. Attention intact, able to perform serial sevens correctly. Recalls 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. No confusion or post-ictal features noted at time of examination.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus. CN V: Sensation intact in all three divisions bilaterally. Masseter and temporalis strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation. CN VIII: Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, gag reflex intact. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline without fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk throughout. Tone normal in all extremities without rigidity or spasticity. Strength 5/5 in all muscle groups bilaterally: deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, finger abductors, grip, hip flexors, quadriceps, hamstrings, ankle dorsiflexors, and plantarflexors. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Proprioception intact at bilateral great toes. Vibration mildly diminished at bilateral great toes consistent with mild peripheral neuropathy in the setting of diabetes.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. No intention tremor. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady with normal base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg sign negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration or evidence of oral trauma. No signs of urinary incontinence. No bruising pattern to suggest prolonged tonic-clonic activity. Small abrasion on right temple from fall during syncopal event.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden collapse while watching television at home, witnessed by wife
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Roberto Delgado is a 72-year-old man with a history of coronary artery disease status post two-vessel CABG, hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, and known mild aortic stenosis who presents to the emergency department via EMS after a witnessed syncopal episode at home. The patient was sitting in his recliner watching television at approximately 6:30 PM when he suddenly became pale and slumped to one side, sliding off the chair onto the floor. His wife, who was sitting nearby, states that the episode occurred without any preceding cry, warning, or abnormal movements. She specifically reports that there were no rhythmic jerking movements, no tonic stiffening of the limbs, and no shaking of any kind. She describes him as appearing ashen and motionless for approximately 15 to 20 seconds before he regained consciousness. Upon awakening, he was immediately coherent and oriented, asking his wife why she looked worried. There was no period of confusion, disorientation, or drowsiness after the event, no tongue biting, and no urinary or fecal incontinence. He sustained a small abrasion on his right temple from striking the armrest of the chair. The patient reports that over the past six weeks he has experienced progressive fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, and three prior episodes of near-syncope characterized by a sudden sensation of the room going dark and feeling as though he might lose consciousness, each lasting five to ten seconds and resolving spontaneously. He did not lose consciousness during these prior episodes. He has also noted occasional breathlessness when climbing stairs and vague chest tightness with exertion over the past month, which he attributed to aging. He recalls a minor slip and fall in his bathroom approximately four months ago without loss of consciousness or significant injury, which he mentions as the only prior fall. He denies palpitations immediately before the event, recent fever, headache, visual changes, or focal weakness. His wife confirms he has never had a seizure and that this event looked nothing like a seizure.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-modulated, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Brief drowsiness captured with symmetric vertex waves, no abnormal sleep architecture

artifacts.description: Intermittent low-amplitude muscle artifact, does not obscure underlying cerebral activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Mild symmetric slowing during hyperventilation, returning to baseline within 1 minute of cessation. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at flash frequencies 1-30 Hz. Normal photic driving response at 8-14 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. No epileptiform discharges, seizures, or focal abnormalities identified during this 30-minute routine recording. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording. A single routine EEG has approximately 50% sensitivity for detecting interictal epileptiform discharges; prolonged monitoring would increase sensitivity if clinical suspicion remains.

ecg.rhythm: Complete heart block (third-degree AV block) with junctional/ventricular escape rhythm
findings: Complete AV dissociation with independent atrial rate of approximately 82 bpm and ventricular escape rate of 38 bpm
Wide QRS ventricular escape complexes with right bundle branch block morphology
Left axis deviation consistent with left anterior fascicular block
No acute ST elevation to suggest acute STEMI
Mild ST depression in lateral leads V5-V6, likely rate-related or demand ischemia
Borderline QTc prolongation at 492 ms in setting of bradycardia
ecg.interpretation: Third-degree (complete) atrioventricular block with slow ventricular escape rhythm at 38 beats per minute. Complete AV dissociation is confirmed by P waves marching independently through QRS complexes at a faster atrial rate. The escape rhythm has a wide QRS with RBBB morphology and left axis deviation, indicating an infra-Hisian block. This is a high-risk ECG finding that provides a clear explanation for the syncopal episode and necessitates urgent cardiology consultation with preparation for temporary pacing and subsequent permanent pacemaker implantation.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL (L), BUN 29 mg/dL (H), Creatinine 1.5 mg/dL (H), Glucose 138 mg/dL (H), Troponin I 0.05 ng/mL (H), BNP 312 pg/mL (H), HbA1c 7.4 % (H).

Echocardiogram.value: Moderate sclerosis with mild stenosis, peak velocity 2.8 m/s, mean gradient 18 mmHg

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Left ventricular ejection fraction 42% % (H), Left atrial diameter 4.1 cm (H), Interventricular septum thickness 1.3 cm (H), Aortic valve Moderate sclerosis with mild stenosis, peak velocity 2.8 m/s, mean gradient 18 mmHg qualitative (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Aortic valve: Moderate sclerosis with mild stenosis (H)

output.rhythm: Persistent complete heart block with ventricular escape rhythm throughout 24-hour recording
findings: Persistent complete AV block throughout entire 24-hour recording
Average ventricular escape rate 37 bpm (range 30-44 bpm)
Atrial rate averaging 80 bpm, entirely in sinus rhythm throughout recording
Minimum ventricular rate 30 bpm at 02:18 AM with 3.8-second RR pause
Maximum ventricular rate 44 bpm during brief walking
Isolated PVCs: 18 total in 24 hours, no couplets or runs
output.interpretation: Critically slow rates overnight with pauses up to 3.8 seconds. No intermittent return of AV conduction, indicating a fixed, irreversible conduction disturbance.

Tilt_table_test.value: Presyncope with lightheadedness and visual graying at 3 minutes 30 seconds, test terminated

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Baseline supine HR 38 bpm (H), 70-degree tilt BP at 3 min 90/52 mmHg (H), 70-degree tilt HR at 3 min 39 bpm (H), Symptoms during tilt Presyncope with lightheadedness and visual graying at 3 minutes 30 seconds, test terminated qualitative (H).

output.rhythm: Persistent complete heart block with no increase in ventricular rate during exercise
findings: Test terminated early at Stage 1 of Bruce protocol after 2 minutes due to symptomatic hypotension and presyncope
Peak ventricular rate 42 bpm (severely blunted chronotropic response due to complete heart block)
Atrial rate increased appropriately to 118 bpm but with persistent AV dissociation
Blood pressure fell from 106/58 to 84/48 at peak exertion
1.5 mm ST depression in leads V4-V6 at peak exercise, suggestive of demand ischemia from severe bradycardia
output.interpretation: Exercise stress test severely limited by symptomatic hypotension and presyncope in the setting of complete heart block. Complete chronotropic incompetence with fixed ventricular rate despite appropriate sinus node acceleration.

findings.location: Basal interventricular septum extending to membranous septum

signal_characteristics.T1: Elevated native T1 values in basal septum (1120 ms, normal <1050 ms)
signal_characteristics.T2: Normal T2 signal, no acute edema
signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Mid-wall linear late gadolinium enhancement in the basal interventricular septum at the level of the bundle of His, consistent with non-ischemic fibrosis
Thin subendocardial enhancement involving less than 25% of wall thickness, consistent with prior ischemic injury

additional_observations: Aortic valve calcification with restricted leaflet motion
No evidence of arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy
No evidence of cardiac sarcoidosis or amyloid infiltration
CABG grafts appear patent on available sequences
output.impression: This provides a clear structural substrate for the complete heart block. Additionally, there is a small area of subendocardial scar in the anteroseptal wall from prior ischemic injury. LVEF is moderately reduced at 42%. Progressive aortic valve calcification is noted which may contribute to conduction system disease. No evidence of sarcoidosis, amyloidosis, or ARVC. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: cardiac syncope complete heart block management pacemaker

results.title: 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline on the Evaluation and Management of Patients With Bradycardia and Cardiac Conduction Delay
results.source: Journal of the American College of Cardiology, 2019; 74(7):e51-e156
results.relevance: Directly applicable - Class I recommendation for permanent pacing in symptomatic complete heart block

results.title: Syncope due to cardiac arrhythmias: diagnosis and treatment
results.relevance: Review of cardiac syncope evaluation including the role of ECG, Holter monitoring, and electrophysiology studies

results.title: ESC Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of syncope
results.relevance: Comprehensive syncope guidelines including risk stratification and management algorithms

output.summary: Current guidelines (ACC/AHA/HRS 2019, ESC 2018) provide a Class I, Level of Evidence B recommendation for permanent pacemaker implantation in patients with symptomatic third-degree AV block. Complete heart block with syncope is considered a high-risk presentation requiring urgent intervention. Dual-chamber (DDD) pacing is preferred over single-chamber ventricular pacing. In patients with reduced LVEF (<50%) and anticipated high ventricular pacing burden (>40%), cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT) should be considered to prevent pacing-induced cardiomyopathy. Beta-blockers and other AV nodal blocking agents should be discontinued. Temporary pacing (transcutaneous or transvenous) may be needed as a bridge to permanent device implantation.

interactions: Atenolol is a beta-1 selective adrenergic blocker that suppresses AV nodal conduction and reduces heart rate
In the setting of complete heart block, atenolol may further suppress the ventricular escape rhythm, worsening bradycardia and hemodynamic compromise
Concurrent use with amlodipine (calcium channel blocker) may have additive negative chronotropic and dromotropic effects
contraindications: Complete heart block without a functioning pacemaker is a contraindication to beta-blocker use
Atenolol should be immediately discontinued in this clinical setting
warnings: Abrupt discontinuation of beta-blockers can cause rebound tachycardia and hypertension; however, in the setting of complete heart block with escape rhythm, the risk of continued use far outweighs the risk of withdrawal
Monitor closely for hemodynamic instability after discontinuation
output.formulary_status: Available, but contraindicated in current clinical context
alternatives: No beta-blocker alternative is appropriate until a pacemaker is implanted
After pacemaker implantation, atenolol or an equivalent beta-blocker may be reconsidered for blood pressure management
Consider ACE inhibitor dose adjustment or addition of hydralazine for interim blood pressure control if needed

findings.location: Periventricular and subcortical white matter, bilateral

additional_observations: No enhancing lesions after gadolinium administration
Minimal cortical volume loss appropriate for age
output.impression: Brain MRI is unremarkable for acute intracranial pathology. Mild chronic small vessel ischemic disease appropriate for the patient's age and cardiovascular risk factor profile (Fazekas grade 1). No acute infarction, hemorrhage, demyelinating lesion, or mass. Brain MRI does not reveal a neurological cause for the syncopal episode. Clinical correlation recommended.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to complete (third-degree) atrioventricular block

differential.key_distinguishing: No tonic-clonic activity, no post-ictal confusion, no tongue bite, no urinary incontinence, normal EEG. Wife explicitly states the event looked nothing like a seizure.
No identifiable trigger such as pain, prolonged standing, or emotional stress. No prodromal nausea or diaphoresis. Age and extensive cardiac history strongly favor a cardiac etiology.
Syncope occurred while seated, not upon standing. On antihypertensives but no positional component. Any orthostatic changes are secondary to fixed low heart rate from complete heart block.
No focal neurological deficits on exam. TIA very rarely causes true loss of consciousness. Normal neurological examination and normal brain MRI.

Known mild aortic stenosis, but the degree is not severe enough to explain syncope. ECG-documented complete heart block provides a more definitive explanation. The AS may contribute to conduction disease progression.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately upon emergency presentation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Complete heart block with ventricular escape rhythm at 38 bpm

optimal_actions.action: Initiate continuous cardiac telemetry monitoring and establish IV access
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Persistent bradycardia with complete AV dissociation

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive laboratory studies: CBC, BMP, troponin, BNP, coagulation, TSH, magnesium
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No metabolic cause for syncope; troponin mildly elevated (demand-related); BNP elevated

optimal_actions.action: Immediately hold atenolol and amlodipine to avoid further suppression of AV conduction and heart rate
Apply transcutaneous pacing pads and have atropine and isoproterenol available for symptomatic bradycardia
Obtain EEG to rule out epileptiform abnormalities given transient loss of consciousness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG with no epileptiform discharges

optimal_actions.action: Urgent cardiology consultation for temporary pacing and permanent pacemaker planning
Obtain transthoracic echocardiogram to assess structural heart disease and guide device selection
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduced LVEF, aortic stenosis with valve calcification, left atrial dilation

optimal_actions.action: Admit to telemetry unit or CCU for monitoring pending pacemaker implantation
Plan permanent pacemaker implantation; consider CRT-P given reduced LVEF and anticipated high pacing burden

critical_actions: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately to identify complete heart block
Initiate continuous cardiac monitoring with telemetry
Immediately hold atenolol and all AV-nodal blocking agents
Apply transcutaneous pacing pads and have emergency pacing equipment ready
Recognize that the syncope is cardiac in origin and not an epileptic seizure
contraindicated_actions: Initiating antiepileptic drugs for syncope misdiagnosed as seizure
Continuing beta-blockers (atenolol) or calcium channel blockers (amlodipine) that suppress AV conduction
Discharging the patient without cardiac monitoring and pacemaker evaluation
Performing lumbar puncture (no indication and would delay definitive management)
Administering IV adenosine (would catastrophically worsen AV block)
Ordering carotid ultrasound as primary workup (syncope is not TIA)
key_reasoning_points: Sudden, unheralded loss of consciousness without warning or prodrome while seated is characteristic of cardiac syncope (Stokes-Adams attack) rather than vasovagal syncope or seizure
The absence of tonic-clonic movements, post-ictal confusion, tongue biting, and urinary incontinence argues strongly against epileptic seizure
Immediate return to full alertness and orientation after the event is classic for cardiac syncope; seizures typically have a post-ictal period of confusion
The ECG showing complete heart block with ventricular escape at 38 bpm provides the definitive diagnosis and mechanism for syncope

The patient's history of coronary artery disease with prior CABG, aortic stenosis with valve calcification, and hypertensive heart disease all predispose to degenerative conduction system disease (Lenegre-Lev disease)
Atenolol (beta-blocker) and amlodipine (calcium channel blocker) may worsen bradycardia and must be immediately discontinued
The normal EEG confirms the non-epileptic nature of the transient loss of consciousness
Elevated BNP and mildly elevated troponin reflect chronic cardiac dysfunction from sustained bradycardia, supporting the urgency of pacemaker implantation

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward presentation with classic features of cardiac syncope: sudden unheralded loss of consciousness, no seizure features, immediate recovery, and clear ECG finding of complete heart block with slow escape rhythm. Minimal confounders.
clinical_pearls: Complete heart block is a Class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation regardless of symptoms
Stokes-Adams attacks (syncope from transient cardiac standstill or severe bradycardia) present without warning and with rapid recovery
Lenegre-Lev disease (degenerative fibrosis of the conduction system) is the most common cause of acquired complete heart block in the elderly
Aortic valve calcification can extend into the adjacent conduction system, causing progressive AV block
Beta-blockers must be immediately discontinued in complete heart block without a pacemaker
